focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 2

The light-bill money rode under Marcia's left bra strap all through service, the folded cash warming against her skin until the paper edges softened and curled. By the second hymn it felt less like money than like a hot leaf pasted to her body, something fragile that would tear if she breathed wrong. She kept one hand flat on her pocketbook anyway. The pocketbook held the tithe envelope, two safety pins, a pack of peppermint she had meant to ration for Micah, and the red copy of the code notice folded so small it could almost pass for something manageable.

Church was full because people liked to look at a child who had done well and then look at the people who had made her. Tiana sat straight in the pew with her graduation hair wrapped down into a neat puff, the gold clip at one side catching the weak light from the high windows. The girl had changed out of the dress from Friday night, but the room was still treating her as if she were carrying a crown in her lap. Women leaned in to squeeze her hand. Men nodded at Delroy with that public pride fathers got to wear cheaply. Micah sat on the aisle in a shirt that had belonged to a cousin first and then somebody from church before it reached him. He kept two fingers hooked under the collar and tugged when he thought Marcia was not looking.

She was looking at all of them. Church made that seem holy. Everybody else was looking too.

The sermon was from Matthew, the bit about wise men building on rock and foolish men on sand. Pastor Green had a gift for making old warnings sound as if God had typed them that morning and slipped them under your own door. Every time he said house, Marcia saw the warped side wall of the garage apartment and the thin crack above the bathroom window that Delroy kept calling cosmetic....
